plainMeaning: This verse simply recites the names of more great women of unshakeable virtue — Brāhmī, Sundarī, Rukmiṇī, Revatī, Kuntī, Śivā, Jayantī, Devakī, Draupadī, Dhāriṇī, Kalāvatī and Puṣpacūlā — so that we remember them and try to live as nobly as they did.
recitation: baṁbhī sundarī ruppiṇī, revaï kuntī sivā jayanti ya; devaï dovaï dhāriṇī, kalāvaï pupphacūlā ya.

wordByWord.gloss: Brāhmī (elder daughter of Ṛṣabhadeva, the first Tīrthaṅkara)
wordByWord.etymology: Prākrit baṁbhī ← Sanskrit brāhmī, 'belonging to Brahman/sacred learning'; tradition credits her with the Brāhmī script.
wordByWord.notes: With her sister Sundarī she renounced and became a sādhvī.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit sundarī, 'the beautiful one', from sundara 'lovely'.
wordByWord.notes: Famed for a long austerity (āyambil tapas) sustained while awaiting permission to renounce.

wordByWord.gloss: Rukmiṇī (chief queen of Kṛṣṇa who turned to renunciation)
wordByWord.etymology: Prākrit ruppiṇī ← Sanskrit rukmiṇī, from rukma 'gold'.
wordByWord.notes: The same name recurs in v.11 among Kṛṣṇa's eight chief queens; recensions differ on whether the two refer to one satī or are listed twice.

wordByWord.gloss: Revatī (the lay devotee who offered Mahāvīra pure food in his illness)
wordByWord.etymology: Prākrit revaï ← Sanskrit revatī, an asterism-name connoting 'wealthy, auspicious'.
wordByWord.notes: Cited in the Bhagavatī-sūtra; tradition holds her devotion earned tīrthaṅkara-nāma-karma for a future birth.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit kuntī, eponym from the Kunti lineage; one of the satīs shared with the wider Indian epic tradition.
wordByWord.notes: Honoured here for steadfastness, as recast in the Jain Pāṇḍava narrative.

wordByWord.gloss: Śivā (the virtuous queen counted among the sixteen satīs)
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit śivā, feminine of śiva 'auspicious, benign'.
wordByWord.notes: Traditionally the queen of Caṇḍapradyota of Ujjayinī.

wordByWord.gloss: Jayantī (the learned śrāvikā who questioned Mahāvīra)
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit jayantī, 'the victorious one', from √ji 'to conquer'.
wordByWord.notes: Of Kauśāmbī; her dialogue with Mahāvīra appears in the Bhagavatī-sūtra. She later took dīkṣā and attained liberation; remembered as a foremost śayyātarī (host of monks).

wordByWord.gloss: and (connective particle linking the first line's names)
wordByWord.etymology: Prākrit ya ← Sanskrit ca, the copulative 'and'.
wordByWord.notes: Metrical line-filler joining the preceding catalogue.

wordByWord.etymology: Prākrit devaï ← Sanskrit devakī, 'the divine/godly one', from deva.
wordByWord.notes: A satī of the Hari-vaṁśa cycle as received in Jain narrative.

wordByWord.etymology: Prākrit dovaï ← Sanskrit draupadī, 'daughter of Drupada'.
wordByWord.notes: Her renunciation and rebirth are told in the Jñātādharmakathā (ch.16).

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit dhāriṇī, 'she who bears/upholds', from √dhṛ 'to hold' — fittingly, 'the steadfast bearer'.
wordByWord.notes: Several canonical queens bear this name (e.g. the mother of Megha-kumāra, queen of Śreṇika); the exact satī intended is not specified. The book reads ધારિણી; some recensions read ધારણી (dhāraṇī).

wordByWord.gloss: Kalāvatī (the satī whose severed hands were restored by the power of her śīla)
wordByWord.etymology: Prākrit kalāvaï ← Sanskrit kalāvatī, 'endowed with the arts/accomplishments', from kalā.
wordByWord.notes: Her story is a classic śīla-māhātmya — false suspicion overturned by chastity's power.

wordByWord.gloss: Puṣpacūlā (the sādhvī who attained omniscience while serving her ailing teacher)
wordByWord.etymology: Prākrit pupphacūlā ← Sanskrit puṣpacūlā, 'crested/crowned with flowers', puṣpa + cūlā.
wordByWord.notes: Disciple of Ārya Aṇṇikāputra; reached kevalajñāna through selfless guru-sevā. Book spelling પુફ્ફચૂલા verbatim.
Seals the second line's list and carries the litany onward into v.11.

literalTranslation: Brāhmī, Sundarī, Rukmiṇī, Revatī, Kuntī, Śivā, and Jayantī; Devakī, Draupadī, Dhāriṇī, Kalāvatī, and Puṣpacūlā.
idiomaticTranslation: Brāhmī and Sundarī; Rukmiṇī, Revatī, Kuntī, Śivā and Jayantī; Devakī, Draupadī, Dhāriṇī, Kalāvatī and Puṣpacūlā — these too we remember.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This gāthā extends the satī roll-call and, read closely, deliberately gathers women from every station of life under the single banner of śīla. It opens with Brāhmī and Sundarī — the very daughters of the first Tīrthaṅkara, who exchanged royal birth for the saṁyama of sādhvīs — and closes with Puṣpacūlā, a nun who reached kevalajñāna not through display but through humble guru-sevā. Between these two poles stand queens (Rukmiṇī, Devakī, Śivā), an exemplary lay devotee (Revatī), a fearless questioner of the Jina (Jayantī), epic heroines absorbed into Jain narrative (Kuntī, Draupadī), the much-praised Dhāriṇī, and Kalāvatī, whose severed hands were restored by the sheer force of her fidelity. The verse advances the panel begun in vv.8–9: where those named earlier are often paired with renunciation-stories, this verse quietly broadens the field — daughter, queen, householder, ascetic — to insist that steadfast virtue is not the privilege of one role. No deeds are narrated; consistent with the sutra's method, the bare name is enough, each syllable a doorway to a remembered life. The two ya particles are not idle metre but the litany's heartbeat, stitching name to name so the reciter's breath itself becomes an act of homage.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse embodies the Jain conviction that the highest spiritual attainments are open to women across every worldly condition. Puṣpacūlā's kevalajñāna and Revatī's tradition-ascribed future tīrthaṅkara-potential testify to women's full capacity for liberation; Brāhmī and Sundarī model right renunciation; Kalāvatī's restored hands dramatize śīla as an active, world-shaping power, not mere passivity. By naming queens beside nuns, the gāthā affirms that fidelity to dharma — not social station — is what makes a satī.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited at dawn, this verse asks today's practitioner to carry a specific exemplar into the day: the householder may hold Revatī's pure-hearted giving, the student Brāhmī's learning, anyone facing slander Kalāvatī's unshaken integrity. The point is imitation, not petition — let one name steady one resolve.

crossReferences.text: Draupadī's renunciation and rebirth — Jñātādharmakathā, adhyayana 16

sources.title: 16 Sati in Jainism (JAINpedia content)
sources.consultedFor: The fixed list of sixteen satīs and confirmation of the sajjhāy's role in the rāī pratikramaṇa; identities of Brāhmī, Sundarī, Śivā, Kuntī, Puṣpacūlā.

sources.title: Bharahesar Sajjhaya — Jain Sutras and Stotras
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim verse-10 text and the count of 47 virtuous women in the litany; variant reading ધારણી/dhāraṇī.

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon (Monier-Williams)
sources.consultedFor: Sanskrit root-glosses for rukmiṇī, revatī, śivā, jayantī, draupadī, dhāriṇī, kalāvatī, puṣpacūlā.

notes: The verse is a pure name-list, so identifications rest on traditional satī lore rather than internal evidence. Specific uncertainties: (a) Ruppiṇī/Rukmiṇī also appears in v.11 among Kṛṣṇa's chief queens — whether this is the same satī honoured twice or a distinct listing varies by recension; (b) 'Dhāriṇī' names several canonical queens (notably Megha-kumāra's mother), and the source does not single one out; (c) Revatī's future tīrthaṅkarahood and Jayantī's biography are tradition-ascribed and reported here as such; (d) book orthography preserved verbatim — ધારિણી (vs the dhāraṇī of some printed texts) and પુફ્ફચૂલા.
